data.title: Pinnacle Financial Partners, Inc. 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Pinnacle Financial Partners, Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Banks Liking These Market Swings
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Bank earnings show strong trading revenue amid big swings. (0:16) HPE draws activist interest. (2:20) Fund managers have never been so worried about one outcome. (5:01)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, banks are making hay while the volatility sun shines, cashing in on a turbulent market.

Following a strong quarterly report Monday from Goldman Sachs (GS), thanks to equity sales and trading, Bank of America (NYSE:BAC) and Citi (C) earnings reports exceeded Wall Street expectations, also showing trading outperformance.

BofA turned in a bottom-line and top-line beats, helped by sales and trading revenue growth at its Global Markets division.

CEO Brian Moynihan said the bank's year-over-year 18% increase in GAAP EPS "reflected growth in net interest income and fee income, while sales and trading delivered its 12th consecutive quarter of year-over-year revenue growth."

But he also said: “We potentially face a changing economy.”

Bank of America sees net interest income on fully taxable equivalent basis rising $15.5 billion-$15.7 billion by Q4 2025 (vs. Visible Alpha consensus of $15.6 billion). That would be up from $14.6 billion in Q1.

Citigroup reaffirmed 2025 guidance and its Q1 earnings and revenue beat estimates, with strong performance in its markets and investment banking businesses.

The bank kept its 2025 guidance unchanged, subject to macro and market conditions. It expects net interest income, ex-markets, up 2%-3% from 2024. Full-year revenue is expected to be ~$83.1 billion to $84.1 billion, compared with its prior view of $83.5 billion to $84.5 billion, vs. the average analyst estimate of $83.6 billion.

Ian Bezek, Investing Group Leader for Ian's Inside Corner, called the results excellent, with equity and fixed income trading results “particularly robust.” But he also noted a "15% rise in year-over-year costs related to potential credit losses, and that trend could accelerate in Q2 thanks to uncertainty from the tariff situation."

Among other active stocks, Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) raised its full-year sales outlook with its Q1 results, while its sector is bracing for new tariffs under the Trump administration.

J&J reaffirmed its full-year EPS outlook of $10.50–$10.70 on a non-GAAP basis while raising its reported sales outlook to $91 billion to $91.8 billion from the $89.2 billion to $90 billion issued three months ago.

Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE) is gaining on a report that activist Elliott Investment Management has accumulated a more than $1.5 billion stake.

Bloomberg says Elliott is expected to engage with the software and networking company to help boost its share price, but specifics aren’t known.

And Albertsons (ACI) is struggling as the grocery chain’s soft EPS guidance offset a better-than-expected profit in the fourth quarter.

For 2025, the company is projecting adjusted EPS of $2.03 to $2.16, falling below the consensus estimate of $2.30 per. Additionally, identical sales are expected to increase 1.5% to 2.5% with adjusted EBITDA of $3.8 billion to $3.9 billion versus 2.0% growth in identical sales and adjusted EBITDA of $4.005 billion in the previous year.

In today’s trading, the major averages are bouncing around in choppy trading.

The S&P 500 (SP500) saw an ominous technical signal flash on Monday with the Death Cross. That’s when the 50-day moving average crosses below the 200-day moving average and, as its name implies, it’s bearish.

The last time that happened with the benchmark index was 3 years ago.

Meanwhile, Societe Generale noted that history has a warning for high-tariff environments.

Two of the biggest tariff eras in U.S. history saw a sharp decline in large-cap earnings.

Strategist Manish Kabra says: "The history of high tariffs shows extreme danger: 1890s McKinley Tariff led ~60% EPS drop in S&P 500; 1930s Smoot-Hawley Tariff led 70% drop."

Check out the chart.

Looking to the economy, the Empire State Manufacturing Index rose to -8.10 in April from -20 in March, compared with the -12.80 consensus.

Pantheon Macro economists Oliver Allen says: “Companies anticipate a sharp downturn ahead, with the expected business conditions index dropping to -7.4, from 12.7, the second-lowest reading since this component’s all-time low, in 2001. The drop in the capex expectations index, to 1.6, from 9.2, means it has now reversed essentially all of its initial jump in late 2024, consistent with a freeze on investment plans.”

“Meanwhile, the prices paid and received components jumped, both to their highest levels since September 2022, another indication of the building upward pressure on core goods inflation from the new tariffs,” he said.

In other news of note, Uber (UBER) and Waymo are readying for the launch of commercial robotaxi services in Atlanta.

Uber is now looking to see how many of its customers in the city are interested in a Waymo robotaxi service.

The two companies said in September 2024 that they were looking to launch in Atlanta and Austin in early 2025. Waymo services started on Uber in Austin in March.

Under the collaboration, Uber handles vehicle maintenance, charging and access management through its app. Waymo monitors the tech, autonomous operations and rider assistance.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, BofA’s April Fund Manager Survey is out with a unique stat. Money managers have never been this worried about a single event.

A potential trade war and the threat of a resulting global recession is the biggest tail risk, with 80% of fund managers surveyed listing it as a concern. That’s the highest level of consensus around a single risk factor since BofA began tracking tail risks 15 years ago.

Beyond the looming threat of a trade war, other concerns, albeit to a far lesser extent, were inflationary pressures prompting aggressive interest rate hikes by the Federal Reserve and a potential crash in the U.S. dollar triggered by a foreign buyers’ strike. Worries about an AI bubble have almost disappeared, at 1%.

For fans of contrarian indicators, this is the 5th most bearish survey in 25 years, which may point to a time to buy.

data.title: Fiscal Flows, Bank Credit, And The Fed For April
data.description:
This article examines the US sectoral flows for March 2025 to assess their likely impact on markets as we advance into April 2025. It also includes an impact assessment of significant Fed and White House decisions and policies on risk asset markets. This is of particular importance as decisions are coming out of the Whitehouse at present that are causing sentiment events so severe that they override the liquid dynamics of the fiscal flows that would otherwise persist and create seasonal repeatable patterns with which to trade.

What happens in March affects what happens in April and often many time frames later. This is due to the lagged impact of fiscal flows on asset markets.

The table below shows the sectoral balances for the US, which are produced from the national accounts.

Source: US Treasury Department, FRED, and author calculations

March is seasonally a good month for fiscal flows, and this year has been an exception with weaker-than-normal federal deficit spending. The bad news is that April is also a bad month often associated with a private sector deficit caused by a federal government surplus budget. March saw a net addition of a solid $152 billion liquidity surplus in the private domestic sector.

Going into detail: The $152 billion private sector funds surplus came from a weaker-than-normal $165 billion injection of funds by the federal government (and this includes the new injection channel from the Fed of around $2.5 billion from interest on reserves that went directly into the banking sector).

The banking sector contributed a very moderate $88 billion. Banking credit has been relatively weak for the past two months. This may be because the debt limit impasse at the White House blocks the creation of treasury securities as collateral for lending and so crimps what the banks can offer within their capital asset ratios. Remember that banks are capital-constrained and not reserve-constrained.

The chart below shows how well private credit markets are performing.

Source: ANG Traders from the Away from the Herd SA investment group

The external sector extracted -$101 billion from the private domestic sector and deposited it into foreign-held bank accounts at the Fed (the external sector X) in return for imported goods and services.

The chart below shows the sectoral balance data plotted in nominal terms. The calculation is federal government spending or G plus the external sector (X and usually a negative factor). The result is how much money enters or leaves the private domestic sector or P, an accounting identity true by definition.

Source: US Treasury and SPX and author calculations

Last month, the green private domestic sector line made a weak movement upward, and this suggested the (SPX) would finish flat to slightly higher in the month, and the result was that the SPX finished 321 points lower. This large decline was associated with the flash crash sentiment event caused by the tariff statements coming out of the Whitehouse, discussed further below in this article.

This month going forward, the model is predicting the SPX should finish the month higher than it began. This is because of the strength of the fiscal flows. This is not a high bar to reach given the low finish mentioned above and the recent semi-reversal of the Trump Tariffs and the consequent V-shaped recovery of the stock market.

Any guidance from financial models such as the one above is extremely limited when there are sentiment events of such magnitude taking place. One thing that can be said is that the fiscal flows are strong enough to support a V-shaped recovery from such an event, and this is a good sign.

The table below shows the total federal government withdrawals from their accounts at the Federal Reserve Bank. A withdrawal by the federal government is a receipt/credit for the private sector and, therefore, a positive for asset markets.

Source: US Treasury Department, FRED, and author calculations

The table shows that total Federal government outlays made another decrease month over month and are now moving in the $2 trillion+ range. This month, we had a negative red flush in that all fiscal factors were negative. The federal deficit was much smaller than usual for March and points to weak risk asset markets going forward. A red flush is also present in the first table in this article concerning the fiscal flow acceleration and velocity effects and is also not a positive signal.

Federal government policy at present has declared the intent to 'tackle' the US's twin deficits. The federal spending deficit (which is the private sector's surplus) and the trade deficit.

The weaker federal withdrawals and federal deficit numbers show the federal government is spending less and reducing this deficit. The downside of this is that the private sector surplus is also reduced. Financial balances in the private sector are less.

Unlike the federal deficit, the trade deficit really is a financial deficit for the private DOMESTIC sector while at the same time providing a surplus in the form of real goods and services (A simple trade of money for things).

A trade deficit results in dollars leaving the private domestic sector (the US's domestic internal economy, and moving to bank accounts owned by foreigners). The bank accounts of US citizens and businesses go down, and the bank accounts of foreigners go up. Due to strict foreign ownership laws, only about 5% of these export earnings can re-enter the US domestic economy by way of stocks, bonds, or real estate; the rest are stored as Treasury securities and thereby lost to the domestic economy as is the coupon payment from it.

In the abstract, there is a two-sector model at work here. On the one hand, there is the US federal government, and on the other hand, the rest of the world is the private sector. The private sector can be further divided into a subgroup, the US domestic economy known as the private domestic sector and the rest of the world being the external sector.

Lower federal spending and trade tariffs lower the financial balances of the rest of the world and are recessionary.

One way of solving this effective impoundment of this money would be to relax foreign ownership laws so that the US dollar export earnings could be spent back into the US domestic economy. The other way is to stop trading, and another way is to tariff the export earnings out of existence.

If the current account deficit were balanced to zero, it would allow the sectoral balance in the US to improve as follows:

Private sector balance [P] = Federal government spending [G] + Net Exports [X]

For 2024:

P = 6.15% of GDP + (-3.9% of GDP) = 2.25% of GDP.

Let us assume the external sector is balanced for one reason or another.

P = 6.15% of GDP + 0% of GDP = 6.15% of GDP.

This looks great, and one might expect private domestic sector financial balances would almost triple now that all that federal government deficit spending is adding to private domestic sector bank accounts. The private domestic share moves from 2.25% to 6.16% now that 3.9% is not flowing to the external sector.

But there is a huge deficit of real goods and services, and this means shortages and higher prices as market demand bids them up. Domestic factories cannot be built overnight, and the $1.3 trillion dollars of goods and services that were formerly imported become a massive real shortage of goods and services. This means the extra 3.9% of GDP cannot be allocated to the private domestic sector, as it would create too much aggregate demand for goods and services that are not available to be bought.

In all likelihood, what would happen is Federal government expenditures would fall to 2.25% of GDP or, even worse, go to zero in a balanced budget scenario or to a minus scenario if the federal government was running the much-desired surplus budget.

That the elimination of the twin deficits is desirable is a fallacy of composition, as the mathematics shows. If it were achieved, the mathematical and physical result would be that financial balances in the private domestic sector would theoretically eventually run to zero in a surplus budget, and the pile of goods and services that were coming from overseas would no longer add to the US's real wealth.

The actual solution is that one should seek to import as many 9foreign goods and services as they are willing to exchange for treasury IOUs so that you have lots of material wealth AND the federal government should deficit spend to pay for the external sector deficit and also enough that the private domestic sector was operating at full capacity with full-employment and stable prices and the savings desires of citizens satisfied. Present Whitehouse policy is about as far away from this utopian situation as it is possible to be.

Fiscal milestones coming up: The bad news is that there is a large federal tax collection in the middle of April, where personal and corporate tax is paid. The tax drain will be moving in the $200 billion range. This seasonally puts a dent in the stock market one month later.

At the White House, the Trump tax cut legislation is bouncing back between the House and Senate and is currently on its second trip to the House. This back and forth could go on for a long time; however, they are aiming to have it done by Memorial Day.

In theory, the Republicans have a trifecta in that they have a majority in the House and Senate, control the office of the President, and could pass legislation quickly. However, internal divisions within the Republican Party prevent this from happening.

Until then, the debt limit is still in place and is creating a treasury drought. The chart below plots how this has affected markets in the past and how this one may play out.

Source: Mr. Robert P. Balan of the Predictive Analytic Models SA Investor Group

On the chart above, treasuries are shown as a dotted black line. On this chart, there are three debt limit periods shown, highlighted by the oblong boxes. The first dark black oblong box covers the 2021 debt limit, which was followed by the 2022 bear market in stocks [blue line]. This was coincidentally not just a protracted treasury drought but also a time of weak fiscal flows from the federal government and the banking sector. The stock market plunged with the treasury drought and stayed down in a bear market for about a year until fiscal flows improved.

The purple oblong box covers an area where there was a shorter but sharp treasury drought with a background of strong fiscal flows from federal government deficit spending and bank credit creation. There was a stock market plunge; however, it was not as deep as in 2022 and did not last as long. This was because the debt limit induced a treasury drought that took place against a background of strong fiscal flows.

The debt limit-induced treasury drought that we have now is shown in the area covered by the oblong box with the broken black lines. As for the 2021/22 debt limit period, this has been protracted, and the stock market has moved down hard for two reasons and is being led down by the falling operating cash balance [light pink) line and debt held by the public [black slashed line].

The first reason is the same as for the 2021/22 period: the fiscal flows are weak in terms of federal government deficit spending and bank credit creation; the last several months have seen decelerating fiscal flows. The second reason is sentiment. The new Trump government has moved so fast and so hard that it has unsettled market participants. So, the so-called "Liberation Day" on tariffs was a good example, as were the mass sackings and closures of many federal government departments.

Fear and Greed Index Today: 13 (Extreme Fear)

In my opinion, the Trump government appears to be led by hard-line Libertarians.

Libertarian: Anti-government advocates of deregulation to disable the public ability to tax and govern finance, real estate and other rent-seeking. The effect is to centralize planning in the hands of the financial sector – Wall Street and its satellites in the City of London, the International Monetary Fund [IMF] and the European Central Bank [ECB]. The aim of libertarian planning is privatization, leading to economic polarization, oligarchy, debt peonage and neofeudalism. What the libertarian (that is, financialization) argument leaves out of account is that taxing land rent and other unearned rentier income requires a strong enough government to rein in the vested interests. Opposing government has the effect of blocking such public power. Libertarianism thus serves as a handmaiden to oligarchy as opposed to democracy.

Hudson, Michael. J IS FOR JUNK ECONOMICS: A Guide To Reality In An Age Of Deception (p. 209). ISLET/Verlag. Kindle Edition.

As naive true believers, it is possible the administration will not raise or remove the debt limit just to see what happens. An attitude that I have heard more than once on this subject from government officials is along the lines of 'let's try it and see what happens'. The idea is to reduce the size of the federal government by defunding it.

The Liberation Day tariff debacle is an example of the naive, extreme economic approach taken. If this were applied to the debt limit, the result would be more serious than the tariffs debacle. This is because, at the macro level, the US external sector only accounts for about 4% of GDP, leaving the other 96% unaffected. Big headlines, big stock market drop but only a tiny portion of GDP and this is covered in this article in more detail.

As shown above, federal government deficit spending represents 6.15% of GDP and provides the treasury collateral that allows the banking sector to extend loans. If you cut off one, you cut off the other en passant. The federal budget impacts 100% of the US domestic economy and the markets located in it.

Even if the new budget with debt limit relief is passed, the proposed budget is so meager that a strong market rally cannot be expected from it. There will be a relief rally as the flush of blocked treasuries are issued, and this could be traded. However, the budget itself does not propose much in the way of big spending. I have reviewed the document, and in my opinion, it is a 'rob the poor and give to the rich' budget. It has large budget cuts for most forms of welfare that make fiscal space for a continuation of past tax cuts due to expire.

It is likely to continue the ongoing trend of ever weaker fiscal flows that have so far been the hallmark of the Trump presidency.

Source: Mr. Robert P. Balan of the Predictive Analytic Models SA Investor Group

The chart above is a zoomed version of the one before. Important to note is the thick blue line. This line is the change rate of debt issuance. One can see how the change rate of the SPX [orange line] follows the blue line, as does the change rate of Bitcoin (GBTC). As the chart notes above, "as the change rates rise, stock, and crypto markets rise as well… and vice versa."

All the lines are led by the change rate of Fed Aggregate Credit [light blue line], which is set to move all markets downward.

From a nominal first derivative perspective, the (SPX) dull green line cannot move upward and make new highs in an environment where the debt held by the public [purple line] does not rise. It is range-bound by the lack of liquidity and vulnerable to weakness when a bad sentiment event comes.

This situation gets worse when one looks at the big macro world picture at the end of this article.

The Federal Reserve concluded its March 2025 meeting, leaving unchanged its overnight borrowing rate in a range between 4.25%-4.5%.

The next meeting is at the end of this month, and it will be interesting to see what they do, given the new data and volatile times.

The following chart plots key indicators used to estimate what the Fed might do next, and has proven very predictive.

Source: Mr. Robert P. Balan of the Predictive Analytic Models SA Investor Group

The federal funds rate [FFR] is led by a multitude of co-factors. This month, all the co-factors are pointing to the Fed lowering the policy rate. From the top of the legend to the bottom:

1. The green GDP line is falling.

2. The pink change rate of the 10-year bond yield has fallen, as has the nominal purple line.

3. The black broken SPX line has fallen, as has the nominal orange line.

4. The brown unemployment line, which is inverted, has fallen, meaning more unemployment.

The economy is failing, and the Fed may well lower the FFR to address growth issues. All the data is there for a lowering of rates, and any decision not to will be political rather than technical. Often, central bank mandarins will seek to discipline federal governments by exposing them to bond yield movements they could otherwise have controlled. The British prime minister, Liz Truss, was ejected from office by the lack of action of her central banker, who could have at all times controlled the yield curve anywhere he chose to have it by the buying and selling of bonds on the central bank's market account. The bond market allegedly showed its displeasure at her new federal budget, and the Central Bank of England let it happen. This happened to Trump when bond yields ticked up several days into Liberation Day, as the chart below shows.

US 10-Year Treasury Bond Note Yield - Quote - Chart - Historical Data - News

On the chart above, the orange ovals show where the bond market disciplined Trump with the threat of more expensive national debt servicing in the future. The bond rate whipped up, suggesting that the Treasury must issue higher premium treasury securities in the future. This increases the Federal government deficit and national debt, which is seen incorrectly by the mainstream as bad. In mathematical fact the treasury interest adds to private sector income and the larger national debt increases the money supply and adds to financial sector balances after the following accounting identity correct by definition:

Federal Government Deficit = Private Sector Surplus

My opinion is that Congress needs to come together and take away Presidential discretion regarding the emergency use of tariffs (what emergency) and place this power firmly with Congress.

The Trump 1 presidency indicated this was a problem, though tariffs on China introduced under Trump (2018) were reviewed and ultimately maintained by the Biden administration and in some areas enlarged. But this was done incrementally, without the showman's shock and awe tactics being used now.

I believe the Trump 2 presidency shows how the tariff power lever can be abused. Congress had four years to close this loophole during the Biden presidency but did not do it, and now the bill has come in to be paid. Now that the hammer has fallen, there is renewed talk in Congress to pass such a bill.

“Trade wars can be devastating, which is why the Founding Fathers gave Congress the clear Constitutional authority over war and trade,” Cantwell wrote in a statement Thursday. “This bill reasserts Congress’s role over trade policy to ensure rules-based trade policies are transparent, consistent, and benefit the American public.”

Source: Senator Cantwell.

At the world macroeconomic level, we have the G5 and C5 chart below. The chart shows the spending level by the top five world governments (the G5) in a change rate format together with a range of risk assets such as gold (GLD), (DXY), and the stock market (SPX). The C5 are the balance sheets of the five largest central banks.

Source: Mr. Robert P Balan of the Predictive Analytic Models SA Investor Group

Economic outcomes are preprogrammed from spending patterns that happen up to 18 months before. The brown and blue lines are fiscal carrier waves along which other smaller fiscal impulses resonate.

On the chart above the brown G5 line, expenditures of the world's largest federal governments lead asset markets such as (GBTC) and other crypto and the stock market (MSCI) by about 18 months, as does the blue line of the aggregate balance sheets of the largest central banks, the C5.

At this scale, this is a scary chart. The big strategic rollover of G5 and C5. Note the air pocket of G5 and Chinese credit impulse [green broken line] right about now. The stock market weakness was preprogrammed, and Trump made a gradual decline rout with his tariff statements.

The chart below is the same information as above but zoomed in with more details, down to a quarterly basis.

Source: Mr. Robert P Balan of the Predictive Analytic Models SA Investor Group

If you have not been paying attention to this model of Global Liquidity, I believe it is essential to pay attention to it now.

The model says we have an inflection point higher in risk assets until May 2025 -- then the floor caves in. Bad times until August 2025. The cyclical downturn is NOT over yet. Q4 2025 will be a superb up-cycle for risk assets -- then the entire floor system gives way -- and no stopping until Q1 2027.

This model is always available, always live at the PAM Analytics (First Principles) channel 24x7.

Source: Mr. Robert P Balan of the Predictive Analytic Models SA Investor Group Forum chat room.

All of this needs to be seen from behind the lens of the law of rent that drives the land cycle on an even longer 20-year time frame.

Law of Rent

The law of rent states that land captures all the gains.

No matter what improvement is made to make the economy more productive and free more income for other things, it ends up flowing into the locational value of land. It is to economics what the law of gravity is to physics.

(Source: Mr Phil Anderson, Property Share Market Economics)

United States FHFA House Price Index

This is the chart to watch. The chart is for the US housing price index. The green circles highlight what is known in land cycle theory as a mid-cycle crisis.

The two green circles from left to right are more commonly known as the 2000 Dot Com crash and the 2020 COVID crash. Here, the stock market went down, but the land price did not. That is the key point. They were serious falls, but not threateningly systemic to the finance system.

The red circles highlight what is known in land cycle theory as land-led recessions. The important difference is that at the time shown circled green, the land price was unaffected and kept rising. In the areas shown circled in red, the land price rolled over and went down.

From left to right, the first red circle is the Global Financial Crisis [GFC] of 2006-09. The second red circle is GFC 2, which is still in development.

At the moment, the land price is still rising, so from a land cycle perspective, there is no problem. See the red arrows next to the red circles. In the current cycle, the arrow is still rising and at a steep gradient; it has not flattened, which is the first warning, and it is definitely not going down yet.

In 2006, the first red circle, the land price went down and did so for four years of falling property prices and this is what gave us the financial crisis because the banks lost so much equity as the asset backing for their loan liabilities lost value.

This is the accounting formula:

Equity = Assets - Liabilities.

Mortgages collateralized against properties are the largest part of the credit capital structure, and when property values fall, it brings down the mortgage market and creates a run on the banks, and you get a land-led recession with a serious financial crisis because liabilities exceed assets as the asset prices fall.

The land cycle peak and rollover are not here yet, but it is getting close, and now is the time to reduce real estate exposure and leverage.

The chart below shows how this cycle has been repeating since 1955.

The table below shows how this cycle has been repeating since 1902.

Source:

Akhil Patel. The Secret Wealth Advantage: How you can profit from the economy’s hidden cycle (p. 410). Harriman House. Kindle Edition.

Please like, share, and subscribe.

data.title: PepsiCo Q1 Preview: This Isn't The Time To Chase PEP's Dividend
data.description:
Investment Thesis

If you look at the dividend yield, PepsiCo (NASDAQ:PEP) might seem like a perfect long-term buy. The stock suggests a 3.75% yield, 52 years of dividend growth, and consistent quarterly payouts. Sounds like exactly the kind of stock you’d want to hold through a downturn in the market, especially given the recession stays a strong possibility later this year. However, I believe this is one of those cases where you have to look deeper to fully understand what to expect from the stock before investing in it, particularly with earnings just around the corner.

Right now, I don’t think it’s the best time to chase PepsiCo’s dividend. If I were considering a defensive position, I’d rather look at other stocks in the sector that show better momentum or stronger fundamentals going into earnings.

Company Overview

PepsiCo is known globally for its beverage and snack portfolio, with a presence in nearly every grocery store. It’s long been viewed as a steady, defensive stock, especially popular with dividend investors, and not without reason. The company has a 52-year history of dividend growth, which is an insane amount of time compared to the 2 years for the sector on average.

PepsiCo’s Dividend

PepsiCo’s 3.75% dividend yield is about 25% higher than the sector median. It’s also well above its own five-year average of 2.9%. For dividend investors, that looks like a great entry point. But once you dig into the payout metrics, the picture changes slightly.

One thing that stood out to me is the Dividend Safety Grade of A-. At first sight, that looks reassuring, but once you break down the numbers, it doesn’t really add up. Most of the underlying metrics are actually weak: the dividend coverage ratio is rated D, total debt-to-equity sits at an alarming 262%, and the cash dividend payout ratio exceeds 100%.

The only reasons for an A- rating are backward-looking metrics like strong cash from operations and high return on equity. PepsiCo is profitable and generates a lot of cash, without a doubt, but when the payout structure is already stretched, it doesn’t take much of a slowdown to create pressure. It doesn't mean that something will happen to the company or even to the dividend. Usually, dividend giants like this have a few tricks up their sleeves to ensure some dividend growth, even if it’s minimal, but I wouldn’t call it a perfect situation either.

Valuation: Cheaper, But Not Cheap Enough

Even after a year of underperformance, PepsiCo is not trading at a bargain. PEP stock is down ~14% in one year, while S&P 500 (SP500) returned about 3%.

Its forward non-GAAP P/E stands at 17.46, roughly 12% above the sector median. The PEG ratio (price to earnings divided by growth) is very high: 3.69 compared to 2.34 for the sector. It means that the company doesn’t show the growth it was supposed to show, given the premium valuation.

It is true that PEP is trading ~25% below its 5-year average valuation multiples, including P/E and EV/Sales. But that discount reflects the market’s lower expectations. There’s no real margin of safety here unless you believe the company is about to surprise to the upside, which leads us to the next point, the upcoming earnings.

Wall Street Is Clearly Preparing for Weakness

In the past 3 months, analysts have issued 12 downward EPS revisions for the upcoming quarter and zero upward ones. The same is true for revenue: 8 downward revisions, zero upward.

This is definitely a signal that the company might show weaker than expected results. Factor grades on Seeking Alpha, by the way, confirm the trend: the Revisions grade dropped to a D, and the Growth grade is stuck at D+ with no improvement over the past 6 months.

The company is expected to report Q1 2025 earnings on April 24, pre-market. The current EPS estimate is $1.51 on $17.77 billion in revenue.

Underperformance Tells You All You Need to Know

Besides the future expectations and the possibility for earnings miss, the stock has been underperforming for a while now. PepsiCo (PEP) is down 14.2% over the past year, while the S&P 500 (SP500) is up 3.2%. Coca-Cola (KO) is up almost 21% in the same period. Even the Consumer Staples ETF (XLP), which is not exactly known for high returns, gained over 8%.

It means PepsiCo is seriously underperforming compared to its peers, and even a higher-than-average dividend yield doesn’t make up for this underperformance.

A few weeks ago, I wanted to buy KO stock out of the recession concerns, but after analyzing its performance throughout the recession, I realized it performs pretty much in line with the market during downturns, and it’s a solid play if you are in for the dividend. It wasn't what I was looking for, so I wrote an article stating that KO wouldn’t save you from the recession and rated KO stock as a sell. I noted that I wouldn’t exit if I were in for some time now, and my message was that even the biggest brand like Coca-Cola won’t protect you if the crash comes.

Regarding PepsiCo, I can say that the downside looks more serious than the upside. Yes, the fear in the market could serve as a possible catalyst for the stock price, but the performance of the company itself has a much stronger effect on the stock. The future doesn’t look that optimistic, and while the dividend yield without the question is really attractive, I am not ready to lose 10-15-20% of my money for less than a 4% yearly dividend yield.

Conclusion

PepsiCo is still a great company with iconic brands and a loyal consumer base. But right now, I don’t think the stock deserves the unquestioned confidence. The dividend looks nice on paper, but the safety metrics tell a different story. Valuation is still premium, growth doesn’t justify this valuation, and analysts are cutting estimates, which is a serious sign for the future underperformance of the stock price.

In a different environment, I might have looked at PEP as a stable dividend play. But today, with earnings risk on the table and clear underperformance versus both peers and the market, I think it's better to stay on the sidelines. I’m not saying the stock will collapse, but if it slips another 10% or more, I won’t be surprised. I will be more surprised if the stock price continues moving upward from now on. I rate PepsiCo as a Sell, and if I wanted exposure to defensive names, I’d be looking at some other tickers. For example, I would start with those consumer staples having the highest Quant Ratings.

data.title: Amazon Faces An Existential Crisis In Google Gemini (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I had previously written about how Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) will be one of the primary beneficiaries of the AI era due to their close ties to leading AI lab Anthropic. I argued that AWS's ability to provide frontier Anthropic models would act as a significant barrier to entry for aspiring cloud hyperscalers and neoclouds. My opinion has significantly changed with the rise and growing dominance of Google's (GOOG) Gemini AI, which is now dominating virtually all the important industry-standard AI benchmarks.

With Gemini 2.5 firmly established as the best model by a relatively significant margin, the case can no longer be made that AWS offers the best model. In fact, the inverse is now true, in that the best models will only be accessible at scale through Google Cloud Platform. Given that cloud underpins modern business and AI is an increasingly integral part of enterprises, losing the AI race acts as a double whammy. Not only will AWS and Azure lose direct AI revenue, but they could also lose cloud customers that migrate to Google Cloud solely for its AI offerings.

Google's Gemini Takes The AI Crown

Over the past few years, many had viewed Google as an also-ran in a generative AI space dominated by OpenAI, and to a lesser extent, Anthropic. In fact, many had written off Google as too bureaucratic and/or ideologically captured to ever mount a serious challenge against rivals like OpenAI. Fast-forward to 2025, Google's latest Gemini 2.5 Pro Experimental has not only taken the lead, but done so by a significant margin. Google's latest Gemini model is now in first place on most of the industry-standard benchmarks.

While it is now easy to say that Google's dominance in AI was inevitable given the talent, resources, and hardware that the company possesses, hindsight is 20/20. It was far from certain that Google was going to be competitive with OpenAI given how fast OpenAI and Anthropic were, and still are, progressing. By rapidly taking a lead on the frontier model front, at least in terms of benchmark capabilities, Google poses a serious threat to Amazon's AWS cloud business.

Gemini's Cloud Exclusivity Draws Customers

Currently, Google's Gemini models are only available on its cloud platform. While the company is planning to allow enterprises to run Gemini models on their own data centers, the Gemini models will still not be available on competing clouds. This gives Google Cloud Platform a unique lure, as it incentivizes enterprises that only want the very best AI capabilities to migrate to their cloud service.

Given that cloud spending is a zero-sum game, Google Cloud's competitive advantage on this front directly hurts Amazon. After all, there is very little nowadays that truly allows the major cloud providers to differentiate, given that they are basically just renting out hardware and managed services. If Google can maintain and even grow its lead on the AI front, Google's Cloud platform suddenly stands out in a major way for prospective cloud customers. To make matters worse, even existing AWS customers could consider switching if access to AI is a major corporate objective.

AWS: Amazon's Crown Jewel Is At Stake

While Amazon is still most well-known for its e-commerce business, its real crown jewel is its cloud business. In 2024, AWS generated a staggering $107.6 billion in revenue and $39.8 billion in operating income. Despite the fact that Amazon's e-commerce business is now a ubiquitous part of everyday life in the U.S., AWS is the real profit driver behind the company. As such, any threat to AWS carries massive weight.

Losing high-growth workloads, like AI services, could severely dent AWS's future growth trajectory. What's more, the spillover effect of AWS losing an organization's entire compute workload to Google Cloud purely as a result of second-rate AI would be far more devastating. The revenue lost purely from AI usage would likely be negligible. If Google Cloud begins to peel away at valuable workloads as a result of its superior AI, Amazon's crown jewel AWS would be seriously damaged. Enterprises have already started to switch to Google Cloud Platform in greater numbers. This trend could be supercharged if Google is able to keep or, worse, even widen its AI lead in the industry.

Amazon's Own AI Models Lag Far Behind

Amazon has been developing its own AI models to remain somewhat relevant in the current AI landscape. So far, even their best model, Nova Pro, is barely worth mentioning compared to the frontier models leading the industry. In fact, Nova Pro barely cracks the top 50 on the industry's most widely accepted benchmark, LiveBench. The situation does not get any better for Nova Pro on other popular benchmarks.

This gap in quality has translated to incredibly weak adoption, even among AWS customers. Unsurprisingly, these models have not garnered anywhere near the buzz of other top models like ChatGPT, Claude, and of course, Gemini. In a winner-takes-most market, a model such as Nova Pro has no real use cases. However, it is important to note that Amazon has entered the game incredibly late, so much of its efforts right now are geared toward catching up to frontier players. For now, AWS remains heavily dependent on third-party models, particularly those from Anthropic.

Betting on Anthropic: The Stopgap Solution

To combat the threat of a rising OpenAI-Microsoft partnership, Amazon made several record-breaking $4 billion investments into OpenAI rival Anthropic. In hindsight, Amazon was incredibly lucky that the OpenAI offshoot Anthropic was even present to begin with. If Dario Amodei did not decide to leave OpenAI to found Anthropic, Amazon could have been completely iced out of the AI game, as no other truly competitive alternative to OpenAI existed for Amazon to invest in. However, Amazon is now facing another challenge in that Anthropic appears to be falling behind Gemini.

For a while, Anthropic had the definitive best model for coding in Claude 3.5 and Claude 3.5 Sonnet. As such, Anthropic and OpenAI held a duopoly of sorts on the frontier AI market, in which both Amazon and Microsoft (MSFT) benefited tremendously. However, the rise of Gemini means that Amazon will no longer have access to the most cutting-edge models. In fact, Gemini 2.5 Pro is handily beating all other models across virtually all subject areas, save for the odd one or two.

AI is the New Cloud Battleground

Generative AI has become the primary battleground for the major cloud providers. Given the increasingly powerful capabilities of frontier AI models, dominating the AI layer could determine the rules for the cloud market. Having a plethora of relatively undifferentiated services no longer suffices, as many of these services can be replicated with ease. For instance, popular AWS services like S3, RDS, and EC2 have already been replicated by all the other major clouds. This puts a great deal of pressure on Amazon to match Google or OpenAI on quality. Amazon clearly understands this dynamic, which explains why it has made multiple rounds of record-breaking billion-dollar investments into Anthropic.

Not All Hope is Lost

While it seems clearer by the day that Google is running away with the AI crown, Amazon is still very much in the game. Anthropic has consistently shown that it is able to catch up, and even surpass, current leaders in the AI race. For instance, despite entering the AI game many years after OpenAI, many developers now believe that Anthropic's Claude models are superior for programming. Even if Anthropic starts to fall behind, Amazon has more than enough resources to pump into Anthropic to keep the company competitive. What's more, Gemini's growing dominance is equally as threatening to Microsoft given its reliance on OpenAI, putting it in a similar situation to Amazon. If Gemini continues to outpace Anthropic and OpenAI, we could very well see Amazon and Microsoft team up to challenge Google's dominance. This situation could very well play out, given the existential threat that Gemini poses to both Amazon and Microsoft's cloud businesses. In fact, OpenAI had already previously made an attempt to merge with Anthropic.

On the technological front, there is still a chance that generative AI in its current instantiation hits a wall of progress. In fact, many leading AI research scientists like Yann LeCun believe precisely in this hypothesis. In such a scenario, no one AI will dominate the market, which means that even if a certain model is currently dominating the market, others will likely catch up.

Conclusion: Amazon's Urgent Imperative

The race is far from over, as AWS's vast customer base and resources give Amazon an inherent advantage over all other cloud players. However, Amazon must act swiftly to improve its own AI offering, whether by deepening its partnership with Anthropic or accelerating internal R&D on Nova to prevent Google from eroding AWS's lead. Given the growing Google Gemini threat, Amazon should be downgraded to a hold at its current market capitalization of $1.96 trillion and relatively high P/E of 33. While Amazon is still relatively well-positioned in the AI landscape, the rapid rise of Gemini could throw a major wrench in Amazon's long-term plans.

data.title: Massive Liquidity Drain May Send Stock Prices Even Lower
data.description:
Markets have calmed somewhat, although it isn't clear how long it will last. Today is Tax Day, and there should be a good amount of liquidity sucked out of the market as the Treasury General Account rises sharply and reserve balances decline. At the same time, the reverse repo facility will likely increase as we head into month-end, tightening liquidity in the overnight funding markets.

As liquidity drains from the market and reserve balances fall, overnight rates may rise again. Because of the debt ceiling, the cash balances held at the Treasury General Account have fallen to their lowest level since the spring of 2023. The overnight funding market has seen a lot of volatility lately, with the secure overnight financing rate seeing big rate swings.

Tax Day Is Here

Going back to 2022, the market has seen some pretty sharp declines around Tax days, as the TGA has risen sharply. In April 2022, the S&P 500 (SP500) saw a massive drawdown. Meanwhile, in 2023, the period was marked by sideways choppiness, and in 2024, the index also saw a sizable pullback. How volatile the market gets around tax day depends entirely on the size of the amount of money that gets pushed into the TGA over that time. The tax receipts during 2023 were not as large as those in 2022 and 2024. If history is a guide, and the TGA rises by $350 to $400 billion, it could create a significant drawdown in the equity market.

Additionally, since the Treasury General Account is a liability on the Fed's Balance Sheet, reserve balances fall when the TGA rises, leading to less liquidity in the market. For example, in 2024, when reserve balances fell sharply between mid-April and month-end, the general collateral rate rose from 5.33% to 5.5%, a sign that liquidity was tight in the market.

Tighter Conditions

Over the period April to May 2024, the S&P 500 fell by roughly 5% as liquidity drained and overnight funding markets tightened. This isn't to say that this will be the same, but it is to say that the conditions will be present and that watching these liquidity conditions can provide a great deal of insight into how the equity market performs and where it may be heading.

The one key difference between 2024 and 2025 is that the reverse repo facility is drained at this point, with a balance of about $102 billion as of April 14. In 2024, it was still in the process of draining. As the repo facility declines, it helps to add reserve back into the system and can offset the impacts of a rising TGA, which may have dampened some effects in 2023 and 2024.

It is important to note that liquidity conditions in the market are likely to weaken between now and the end of the month. Typically, we see the reverse repo facility usage increase as we approach month and quarter ends as banks shore up their balance sheets.

For those without access to overnight funding rates, the best thing is to watch the secured overnight funding rate published by the New York Fed every day at 8 AM ET. If it rises, it means liquidity is getting tight, which could mean more rocky times to come.

data.title: Trump Weighs New Tariffs And Auto Sector Relief
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Uncertainty lingers

Market participants remain wary of President Donald Trump's trade policies, with latest updates indicating more tariffs—and rollbacks—may be on the way. Pharmaceutical and semiconductor imports are under scrutiny for potential tariffs, while the automobile sector may be offered some respite in the days ahead.

Review underway: On April 1, the Trump administration began investigating the impact of pharmaceutical and semiconductor imports on national security, likely setting the stage for tariffs on the sectors. The Department of Commerce's pharma investigation covers generic and non-generic drugs, active pharma ingredients, and derivative products. The semiconductor probe covers items including leading-edge chips, chipmaking equipment, silicon wafers, and "downstream products that contain semiconductors."

The probes will look into current and projected demand for the pharma and semiconductor products, the ability of domestic production to meet demand, and the potential for export restrictions. The chip probe will also assess "the ability of foreign nations to weaponize their control over semiconductors and SME supply chains." The investigations were initiated under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962, which allows the president to adjust imports that are found to threaten national security.

Tariff relief? In other news, Trump floated a potential reprieve for the 25% tariffs on automobile and auto part imports, given the industry's deeply integrated North American supply chains. "I'm looking at something to help some car companies with it," Trump told reporters. "They're switching to parts that were made in Canada, Mexico and other places… they need a little bit of time because they're going to make them here." Trump's signal was enough to lift top automakers' shares – GM, F, STLA – which ended on a higher note on Monday.

What else is happening...

WSB survey results: Better trade deals for the U.S. are expected.

Yellen: Bond selloff signals 'loss of confidence' in policymaking.

Consumer polls - Mixed inflation outlook, confidence worsens.

Trump administration to end rule capping credit card late fee.

Lawmakers call on White House to get rid of AI Diffusion rule.

Palantir-NATO deal places it in 'prime position' for AI in defense.

Bristol Myers' (BMY) heart disease drug fails in late-stage trial.

Wall Street-backed crypto bank Anchorage under investigation.

Air travel slumps: U.S. airports see noticeable decline in traffic.

Over $2B of Harvard University's federal funding gets blocked.

Today's Markets

In Asia, Japan +0.8%. Hong Kong +0.2%. China +0.2%. India +2.1%.
In Europe, at midday, London +1%. Paris +0.5%. Frankfurt +1.5%.
Futures at 6:30, Dow +0.2%. S&P +0.2%. Nasdaq +0.3%. Crude -0.6% to $61.17. Gold +0.5% to $3,241.30. Bitcoin +1.3% to $85,757.
Ten-year Treasury Yield +2 bps to 4.39%.

Today's Economic Calendar

08:30 AM Empire State Mfg Survey
08:30 AM Import/Export Prices
11:35 AM Fed's Barkin Speech
07:10 PM Fed's Cook Speech

Companies reporting earnings today »

data.title: trivago: I Think It's Dead Money
data.description:
As tourism normalizes and returns to pre-pandemic levels, many companies see rebounding revenues and margins. Being an online search platform, trivago N.V. (NASDAQ:TRVG) receives positive spillovers of increased travel demand with improved topline performance. However, I have concerns about its business model, which can limit its growth potential. Technical risks are also evident.

trivago’s Decent Closing of 2024

As 2024 closed, trivago N.V. saw the positive impact of improving travel trends and its efforts to rejuvenate or reestablish its brand. This was reflected in its decent performance during Q4 2024. Its operating revenue amounted to €94.8M, a 3.3% YoY growth from €91.7M. This far exceeded its performance for the whole year despite seasonality. It is also worth noting that it appears to be rebounding if we trace the YoY change in its quarterly revenue from 2023. Referral revenue remained its primary revenue component, with growth driven by the Americas and other unspecified regions.

Some contributors were the gradual economic recovery despite the sudden inflation uptick in Q4 2024, decreasing oil and fuel prices, and increased internet users. With trivago’s popularity enhanced by its ties with Expedia, customer loyalty also helped.

Another impressive thing it showed during the quarter was its operational efficiency despite the sticky inflation. As we can see, costs and expenses remained relatively steady. As a result, its operating margin rose from 4.8% to 8.4% YoY.

Drivers

These are some things I believe may help trivago in improving its performance.

Improving Liquidity

One thing I easily noticed is its increasing cash reserves throughout the year, which can indicate increasing cash returns from its operations. True enough, its FCF has improved a lot in the past seven quarters.

Moreover, cash alone is enough to cover all its liabilities in a single payment, making it very liquid. It also has no existing debt. This positions TRVG well amid macroeconomic volatility. This can also be an assurance of its sustainability regardless of its performance.

However, there are some concerns about its Balance Sheet. One is that its book value appears to be shrinking. Its PP&E and intangibles continue to drop, suggesting continued depreciation and that TRVG is slow to replenish or replace its equipment with new ones. While I understand that its business nature is not asset-heavy, it must ensure sufficient and updated equipment and software amid the tighter competition and the AI revolution.

Decreasing Oil Prices

Tourism-related businesses see more glimmers of hope amid the declining price of Brent and WTI. Now, Brent is only $63.7 per barrel, while WTI is $60.5 per barrel. Both are already more than 30% lower than their peak price in 2024. In addition, they are also about half their value during 2022 highs. And I think this can be sustained by the planned OPEC supply hike in the next two months. Increased oil output can further lower global oil and fuel prices.

If this continues, transportation expenses and airfare may be positively affected. This can improve travel spending and demand this year. This can also help stabilize inflation as lower oil prices will affect many industries, especially those using oil and fuel. Aside from airlines and vehicles, lower oil and fuel prices can help restaurants, logistics, shipping, and manufacturing stabilize their costs. If not, it can at least cushion the impact of tariffs on inflation and consumer confidence.

Travel Resilience

In the US alone, travel trends are still showing an optimistic outlook this year. In a study, 92% of Americans may travel this year with a budget nearly double the average amount in 2024. What’s more interesting is that we may expect 56% of Americans to travel more this Spring.

This can be supported by some factors, including decreasing oil and fuel prices and decreased inflation last March. Additionally, hybrid and/or remote work setups allow many people to travel anytime and anywhere they want.

Laggards

These are some of my concerns about trivago.

Business Model

I like its business model for having manageable cost sensitivity and capital intensity. Inflationary headwinds do not necessarily affect their operating cost and expenses.

However, this also poses risks to trivago. One is that its business model is easy to emulate.

In addition, I think it’s lagging behind the market in terms of product or digital innovation as it has only launched its AI Smart Search and personalization recently. And I believe its shrinking fixed assets and intangibles are contributors. Another concern is that it has become easier to market or advertise on social media, which can lower its traffic in the long run.

Increased Competition

Since its business model is easy to establish, we can expect its market space to become oversaturated or crowded. Almost everyone from various sites and apps like Airbnb and even Google competes for the same users.

This can affect its revenue. Although it earns by clicks, not conversions, risks must not be discounted. If these clicks do not result in confirmed bookings, advertiser bids may decrease or even be pulled out. In the long run, the value per click may become eroded, making it hard for TRVG to justify ad spending.

One threat to watch out for is the increasing number of advertisers and evolving ad formats, which can affect the traffic and marketability of advertisements. We can already see its weak performance in its core market, Developed Europe.

Hotels and Airlines as Indirect Competitors

Today, many airlines and hotels allow direct booking on their websites and apps. And as they become more accessible online, many travelers now do direct bookings. This lowers TRVG’s value propositions as direct bookings remove the need for some of its features like price comparisons.

Note that many hotels and airlines promote direct bookings through exclusive rewards like discounts and points. Popular brands like Marriott International, Inc. (MAR), Hilton Worldwide Holdings Inc. (HLT), and InterContinental Hotels Group PLC (IHG) have been leading this trend since the pandemic. After all, this reduces their advertising expense as travelers won’t have to click trivago ads. In addition, many hotels aim to increase customer loyalty.

To conclude this part, I’m not saying all aspects mentioned will lead to trivago’s bankruptcy. To be more realistic, I just believe these risks can shrink TRVG’s value and market share in the long run. As more competitors enter and as direct bookings increase, advertising bids may decrease.

Price Ratio

TRVG has decreased dramatically in the past five years. Yet, I think that’s justifiable considering its key metrics, particularly its book value. As we can see, its price has generally moved along with BVPS. If we try to do a regression analysis from its IPO, we can see a 72% correlation. This shows that a change in BVPS will lead to a 70% chance of the price moving in the same direction. Meanwhile, its p-value of 0.04 shows that only 4% of their relationship happened by chance. Note that I used semi-annual values and did first-order differencing to minimize the impact of seasonality.

If we use the P/B Ratio, the price is not that cheap. It currently trades at 1.24x its BVPS, which is still quite higher than the five-year average. This also suggests a TP of $3.34, justifying the continued price downtrend and showing a $3.34 downside potential.

DCF Model

The DCF Model suggests that TRVG is not that cheap for its intrinsic value. It gives a TP of $3.53, a 2.7% upside potential.

Like most of my coverage on Seeking Alpha, I set its perpetual growth rate at 2% to be consistent with inflation. Meanwhile, I derived its WACC using the Capital Asset Pricing Model. As you can see, it is the same as the cost of equity because TRVG has no borrowings. I also set its risk premium at 5% as my minimum rate to compensate for the risk investors must take if they decide to buy this.

I derived its FCFF by projecting future values of NOPAT, depreciation, capex, and net working capital. I computed its revenue by getting its CAGR for the past five years, which is 9% and used it as the revenue growth rate. Then, I set COGS (2.9%) and operating expenses (95.7%) as a percent of revenue using their five-year average. For depreciation and capex, I computed it using the straight-line method. I didn’t assume the company to buy new equipment to make it simpler. Lastly, I estimated its net working capital by setting its operating assets and liabilities as a percent of revenue. I only used the operating assets and liabilities. Accounts receivables and payables were exceptions since I applied DSO and DPO to them.

Technicals

Technicals show TRVG as a risky option. Its price just fell below the 50-day SMA, showing that recent trends have become a bit pessimistic. Although both are above the 200-day SMA, investors must watch out for a potential bearish reversal.

This can be supported by the bearish divergence spotted last March 21 after setting a new 52-week high. However, buying volume weakened considerably, as shown by MACD and RSI. Their most recent high was lower than the previous one, suggesting uncertainties. True enough, the price took a nosedive, declining by 33% in just three weeks. And I think the trend may continue as it already breached the previous support of $3.97. In addition, it is still above the oversold region. This trend seems to give logic to my target price.

Conclusion

trivago N.V. shows a notable improvement in Q4 with its topline growth and increased liquidity. However, a lot of sustainability concerns must be addressed amid the shifting consumer preferences and tightening competition. In the long run, it may erode its value as more customers and travelers rely more on direct bookings. It is also not worth the risk considering the fact that it’s still not cheap and the evident technical risks. I am giving this a sell rating for not offering added value to your portfolio.

data.title: The World Is Selling America
data.description:
By Brien Lundin

The world is worried. And typically in such an environment, investors across the globe rush to three safe havens: the U.S. dollar, U.S. Treasuries, and gold.

This time is different - because they’re dumping the greenback and Treasuries... and buying gold hand over fist.

This dynamic isn’t just unusual, it’s unprecedented...and illustrated in the chart below.

In this chart, the Dollar Index is in green, the 10-year Treasury yield is in red, and gold, of course, is the gold area in the background. The bottom panel is the 20-day rolling correlation between the dollar and Treasury yields.

In normal times, the dollar and yields are positively correlated, with the correlation line above zero. That brief dip in the correlation in late March merely reflects some back-and-forth, trendless action earlier in the month.

Now note the sharp divergence over the last week when, counter to the Trump administration’s hopes and plans, their harsh tariff policies sent the world running from the U.S.....and toward the one remaining safe haven of gold.

The dollar sank, Treasury yields jumped and the gold price soared.

Put simply, the rest of the world is selling America.

Importantly, as the chart also shows clearly, that flight from U.S. safe havens into gold ended the metal’s brief correction and sent the price skyward, wiping out much of its overbought status in the process.

On Monday, gold was slammed on the U.S. open, as investors flocked back to U.S. equities because President Trump delayed his reciprocal tariffs and exempted electronics exported from China from his most onerous levies.

The steady-state demand from central banks along with investors and institutions around the world is still in place, though, and sparking a rebound from the early-session price lows.

Mining Stocks Take Off

A soaring gold price is nothing new — we’ve seen this kind of action over and over since February of last year.

What’s different this time is that Western investors are now coming to the party...finding the move in gold far advanced...and deciding to play the trend via gold mining stocks.

The result is that, even with gold rocketing higher, the gold stocks have been doing even better. This chart of the GDX gold mining index/gold price ratio shows the dramatic outperformance of the miners.

Even more impressive, the gold stock indices are up again, even with gold down about 1%.

It’s an amazing time - fraught with risks and tremendous opportunities.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: April's 5 Dividend Growth Stocks With Yields Up To 11.99%
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman

For some background on this monthly publication, here is my view on dividend growth stocks:

Dividend growth stocks aren't always the most exciting investments out there. They often aren't grabbing the headlines, and they aren't the stocks running up hundreds of percentages in a year. In fact, they are often some of the least exciting stocks. And that is precisely their strongest selling point. With such a vast world of dividend growth stocks available out there, it is important to screen through to see if there are any worthwhile investments to explore.

They are stocks that provide growing wealth over time to income investors. Dividend growers are often larger (not always), more financially stable companies that can pay out reliable cash flows to investors. Some are slower growers than others. Some are going to be cyclical that require a strong economy. Some are going to be secular, which doesn't generally rely on a more robust economy.

Dividend growth can promote share price appreciation. Of course, that is if these companies are growing their earnings to support such dividend growth in the first place. Trust me. There are yield traps out there - I've owned a few that I'm not particularly proud of.

I like to think of investing in dividend stocks as a perpetual loan of sorts. Essentially, every dividend is a repayment of your original capital. Eventually, holding long enough, you have the position "paid off." It is all returned back into your pocket from that point forward.

All of this being said, it is important to understand my approach to dividend stocks and why screening dividend stocks can be important for income investors. As with any initial screening, this is just an initial dive - more due diligence would be necessary before pulling the trigger.

The Parameters For Screening

I'll be using some handy features that Seeking Alpha provides right here on their website for this screen. In particular, I will be screening utilizing their quant grades in dividend safety, dividend growth and dividend consistency.

Dividend Safety is relatively self-explanatory. These will be stocks that SA quants show reasonable safety compared to the rest of their various sectors. The grade considers many different factors, but earnings payout ratios, debt, and free cash flow are among these. This category will be stocks with A+ to B- ratings.

For the dividend growth category, we have factors such as the CAGR of various periods relative to other stocks in the same sector. Additionally, the quants also look at earnings, revenue, and EBITDA growth. As we will see, this doesn't mean that every stock with a higher grade has the growth we are looking for. This just factors in that the dividend has grown or earnings are growing to support possible dividend growth. For these, the grades will also be A+ through B- ratings.

Finally, for dividend consistency, we want stocks that will be paying reliable dividends for us for a very long time. In particular, hopefully, they are raising yearly, though that isn't an explicit requirement. We will also include stocks with a general uptrend in dividend payments, which means there could have been periods where they paused increases for a year or two.

After looking at those factors alone, we are left with 456 stocks at this time from the 431 listed last month. I'll link the screen here, though it is a dynamic list that constantly updates regularly. When viewing this article, there could be more or less when going to the link.

From there, I wanted to narrow down the list a lot more. I then sorted the list by forward dividend yield, from highest to lowest. Since these will be safer dividend stocks in the first place, screening for those among the higher payers shouldn't hurt. Though there are always exceptions and I try to note those.

Data was pulled as of April 3, 2025—since then, the market has been incredibly volatile, so checking the latest market data before making any investment decision is even more important than usual. Additional due diligence is always required, as this is just an initial screening.

As usual, we have some names that show up quite regularly, and we've provided quick updates in a fairly short period of time. In this case, it is just LyondellBasell Industries N.V. (LYB), which we covered last month.

Further, there are those names that come up on this screen that doesn't necessarily meet the spirit of this piece based on variable dividends or inconsistency when it comes to raising their dividend.

Artisan Partners Asset Management Inc. (APAM) is a name with a variable dividend, for example—while The Western Union Company (WU) is a name that just barely meets the screening for consistency, they actually haven't been raised for years. I also believe that with no growth on the horizon for their business, that isn't likely to change.

That leaves us with NewLake Capital Partners, Inc. (OTCQX:NLCP), RLJ Lodging Trust (RLJ), Hess Midstream LP (HESM), Host Hotels & Resorts, Inc. (HST) and United Parcel Service, Inc. (UPS). These are all names we've touched on before, but it has been at least a quarter.

These are some riskier names currently due to pressures within their industries, that is primarily NLCP and UPS. RLJ and HST are also very sensitive to the overall economy. Given the latest tariff announcements, I'm certainly going to be more somber overall with the current economic outlook. HESM looks like the most solid of the names we'll be looking at today. I wanted to preface this before diving in, so with that, let's look at these speculative names and why they are speculative.

NewLake Capital Partners, Inc. (NCLP) 11.99% Yield

NLCP trades OTC, so already that is a knock against this name. However, getting listed was something they had been working on for quite a while. As of the latest earnings call in early March, though, it appears that the requirements and the costs associated with complying were too great.

Regarding listing, after a careful analysis, we've determined that at this time, the structural requirements and associated costs of pursuing a TSX listing do not justify the benefits. We will, of course, revisit this evaluation if circumstances change, and we know liquidity is critically important, and we will continue to seek options and work hard to enhance liquidity and trading of our stock.

So, it would seem that the idea is on the sidelines for now. Being listed on the NYSE or Nasdaq would provide significant liquidity as there are restrictions on institutional investors in terms of what they can generally invest in.

Further, while NLCP might not be in the worst spot, it's much larger and NYSE-listed competitor, Innovative Industrial Properties, Inc. (IIPR), is having a very tough time with its tenants. This highlights just how risky and speculative this area is. Initially, IIPR's largest tenant was in default and just as those issues were starting to get resolved, more tenants were recently announced as being in default.

I'm long IIPR, but I fully expect a dividend cut at this point, as there was a path previously that could potentially be avoided, but this is probably too much for them to navigate now. This obviously doesn't bode well for NLCP, but on the slightly positive side, NLCP carries nearly no leverage, and similarly, IIPR is lowly leveraged as well.

For now, that ~12% yield remains in place as they declared the same dividend for the first quarter, and it remains quite attractive. Though with tenant issues popping up in its competitor, we are clearly on shaky ground.

RLJ Lodging Trust (RLJ) 7.54% Yield

RLJ is a name we first had on our radar in last November's monthly screening piece. This is a real estate investment trust that is focused on hotel properties, more specifically, on premium branded hotel rooms with "high-margin, focused-service and compact full-service hotels." They are also quite small noting only 96 hotels in 23 states (and DC). They also have an "ownership interest in one unconsolidated hotel with 171 rooms."

As would be expected, this makes them economically sensitive. In particular, during the Covid pandemic, they were hit hard, and that caused them to slash their dividend for a couple of years down to the token penny. In more recent years, they've begun to ramp it back up quite significantly as well.

However, with trade wars on the potential horizon, after the recent tariffs being announced, we could be due for a slowdown. Being that RLJ operates exclusively in the U.S., it might be relatively sheltered in terms of direct impact—it would be more of the indirect impact of the overall economic slowdown that is likely being caused that would be more of a worry.

Hess Midstream LP (HESM) 6.54% Yield

After touching on two more speculative names and two more to go after this, HESM here looks like a solid potential watchlist name. This is a midstream company, and while it is an "LP," there is no K-1. The last K-1 came to investors in 2019, and since they've converted to an up-C structure.

It has come up quite regularly; the last time we touched on this name was last October. Since then, shares have been moving higher, pushing that attractive yield down to ~6.5% from the ~7.20% level that we were at previously.

That is even despite their continued growth of the payout during this period. One appealing part of HESM for dividend growth investors is that they raise their distribution every quarter.

However, like anything in investing, it isn't without its own risks. Midstreams operate in a toll-like way due to mostly fixed-rate contracts. With HESM, though, they have a concentration in that their largest customer by far is Hess Corporation (HES), which accounts for almost 90% of its earnings. Further muddying the waters is the Chevron Corporation (CVX) takeover of HES, which adds more moving parts to consider.

HESM has indicated in a past earnings call that it isn't expected to disrupt their business too much, as the contract structure should remain in place.

In the fourth quarter of 2023, Hess announced that it entered into a definitive agreement to be acquired by Chevron Corporation.

Hess Midstream expects upon consummation of the proposed transaction, Chevron will acquire Hess's 37.8% ownership in Hess Midstream, including its right to appoint four directors to the board of Hess Midstream. Hess midstream's contract structure remains in place.

Host Hotels & Resorts, Inc. (HST) 6.14% Yield

Back to the relatively more sensitive names in terms of pressure from broader economics, we are looking at another hotel-focused REIT. In this case, they note that they are the largest lodging REIT in the world. They are investment-grade rated and note that they own 81 hotels. Of course, that is an even lower number than RLJ, and we noted that they were a small REIT. However, over the 81 hotels, HST has 43,400 rooms—RLJ is less than half of that with 21,200.

Another way to look at this is their enterprise value, with HST being roughly 4.4x larger than RLJ.

Still, even given its size difference, it wasn't enough to be able to withstand the Covid pandemic. This REIT is also quite a bit older, going back to the mid-90s. That gives us plenty of a track record to tell us that HST couldn't handle Covid, but it also couldn't handle the Global Financial Crisis, nor the fallout from the Dot-com bubble—at least in terms of being able to maintain a steady growing dividend. Instead, they slashed it down to nothing during this period.

They also seem to grow to this $0.20 quarterly level, then transition to specials instead of growing it any further. That's normally a solid strategy for a business that is so volatile and uncertain. That can give the management team and the Board some more room to cut back on their specials while maintaining the regulars.

However, as we can see, that strategy hasn't been enough for HST as they actually weren't even able to support the regular during these times. So it's a fairly good theory, but it isn't working out in practice, at least not in this field—the business development company space is an example of an area where it actually tends to work out as intended.

Given that and the expectation for lower AFFO in the coming year before rebounding some, there is some concern here. We aren't probably looking at needing to cut the regular yet, but that will depend on what eventually happens with these tariffs.

United Parcel Service, Inc. (UPS) 5.95% Yield

UPS was a name that wasn't a regular on this monthly screening piece, but over the last six months or so has been very regular due to the share price declines the stock has been experiencing.

UPS is another name that is particularly sensitive to economic conditions and consumers being strong. With fewer packages moving in a slower economy, that's likely to hurt their business. They've already been seeing some significant pressure on their earnings after the pandemic surge.

However, despite the pressures that UPS is facing, they still meet the criteria to be included in today's discussion. They also appear to want to keep that 15-year dividend streak of growth alive as they bumped it up by a penny this year.

Of course, we can also see that they massively raised the dividend shortly after the pandemic, and that might have been getting a bit too ahead of themselves at the time. As we saw right after that, earnings began to decline sharply. Analysts believe that 2024 was the low point, and we could begin to see a turnaround from here.
